coredata.dc:title: Engineering ultrasmall red fluorescent carbon dots for targeted blood-brain barrier crossing, oxidative stress alleviation and mitochondrial repair in cerebral ischemia-reperfusion injury treatment

coredata.dc:description: The alterations in the brain microenvironment caused by reperfusion following acute ischemic stroke are significant obstacles to neurological recovery and a key factor contributing to stroke recurrence after thrombolytic therapy. Elevated levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the brain contribute to both vascular and neuronal damage, underscoring the critical need for the rational design of effective antioxidants with high biocompatibility and efficiency. In this work, we design and engineer a distinct fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots-T7) with specific targeting capabilities by conjugating the T7 peptide (HAIYPRH) for a high affinity to apoptotic neurons. The synthesized C-dots-T7 shows stable red fluorescence with an emission peak at approximately 683 nm, enabling effective in vitro and in vivo bioimaging. In vivo animal experiments demonstrate that the ultrasmall C-dots-T7 (approximately 2–5 nm) efficiently cross the blood-brain barrier, target and accumulate in the ischemic brain tissues, and significantly reduce brain infarct volume by approximately 57.7 % compared to the untreated group in a middle cerebral artery occlusion (MCAO) model. Importantly, the C-dots-T7 can accumulate within mitochondria, activate the oxidative phosphorylation pathway to alleviate mitochondrial dysfunction, and inhibit the mTOR and HIF-1 signaling pathways to prevent excessive cellular damage caused by oxidative stress. This work demonstrates that C-dots-T7 serve as a high-performance nanosystem for fluorescence imaging and targeted treatment of cerebral ischemia-reperfusion injury, with high clinical translation potential.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fluorescence nanozyme C-dots-T7 targeting mitochondria for ischemic stroke therapy. Folic acid and glutathione undergo a hydrothermal reaction in formamide, followed by purification to yield C-dots. The C-dots are then covalently conjugated with T7, forming C-dots-T7. Following intravenous injection, C-dots-T7 nanozymes, designed with ischemic region-targeting capabilities, preferentially accumulate in the ischemic brain lesions, exerting protective effects against ischemic stroke by efficiently scavenging ROS and mitigating oxidative stress.

Preparation and characterization of C-dots and C-dots-T7. (a) High-resolution TEM images of C-dots and C-dots-T7. (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of C-dots with different T7 modification ratios. (c) Zeta potentials of C-dots, C-dots-T7, and T7 (n = 3, independent samples). (d) FT-IR spectra of C-dots, C-dots-T7, and T7. (e) Fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of C-dots-T7. (f) Time-resolved fluorescence curve of C-dots and C-dots-T7 at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm and emission wavelength of 683 nm. (g) SOD-like activity of C-dots and C-dots-T7 at various concentrations. (h) ESR spectrum shows ∙O2 − scavenging activity of C-dots-T7 at different concentrations. (i) ∙OH scavenging activity of C-dots-T7 at different concentrations (n = 3, independent samples). (j) ESR spectrum shows ∙OH scavenging activity of C-dots-T7 at different concentrations. Data are presented as mean ± SD. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001.

In vitro targeting ability and therapeutic effects of C-dots-T7. (a, b) Confocal images and quantitative analysis of SH-SY5Y cells treated with 100 μg/mL C-dots-T7 at different time points (n = 3, independent samples). (c) Confocal images show colocalization of C-dots-T7 with mitochondria in SH-SY5Y cells after 12 h of treatment with 100 μg/mL C-dots-T7. (d, e) Flow cytometric analysis and quantitative analysis of SH-SY5Y cells treated with different concentrations of C-dots-T7 for 12 h post-OGD modeling (n = 3, independent samples). (f) Cell viability of SH-SY5Y cells treated with varying concentrations of C-dots-T7 or C-dots for 24 h (n = 4, independent samples). (g, h) Comparison of intracellular Mito-SOX levels and their quantitative analysis in SH-SY5Y cells treated with different concentrations of C-dots-T7 after OGD modeling (n = 3, independent samples). (i) Effect of different concentrations of C-dots-T7 on SH-SY5Y cell viability following OGD modeling (n = 3, independent samples). (j, k) JC-1 assay results show mitochondrial membrane potential in SH-SY5Y cells treated with different concentrations of C-dots-T7 after OGD modeling, along with quantitative analysis (n = 3, independent samples). Data are presented as mean ± SD. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001.

BBB penetration and ischemic brain targeting of C-dots-T7. (a) Schematic illustration of the therapeutic experiment in MCAO mice. (b) Ex vivo brain fluorescence imaging at different time points following intravenous injection of C-dots or C-dots-T7 in MCAO mice. (c) Distribution of C-dots in bilateral brain regions of stroke-induced mice (n = 3, independent samples). (d) Distribution of C-dots-T7 in bilateral brain regions of stroke-induced mice (n = 3, independent samples). (e) Ex vivo imaging of major organs at different time points after intravenous injection of C-dots or C-dots-T7 in MCAO mice. (f) Biodistribution of C-dots in major organs of stroke-induced mice (n = 3, independent samples). (g) Biodistribution of C-dots-T7 in major organs of stroke-induced mice (n = 3, independent samples). (h) Colocalized fluorescence imaging of C-dots-T7 with vascular endothelial cells in bilateral brain regions of MCAO mice. Data are presented as mean ± SD. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001.

In vivo therapeutic effects of C-dots-T7 on MCAO mice. (a, b) Representative TTC-stained brain slices and quantitative analysis of infarct regions in each treatment group (n = 6, independent samples). (c) Zea-Longa scores on day 3 post-treatment for each group (n = 9, independent samples). (d) TUNEL staining of apoptotic cells in the hippocampal region of brain slices from each treatment group. (e, f) Representative H&E-stained images and quantitative analysis of hippocampal brain sections in each group (n = 3, independent samples). (g, h) Representative Nissl-stained images and quantitative analysis of hippocampal brain sections in each group (n = 3, independent samples). (i) TEM images show mitochondrial ultrastructure in brain tissues across different treatment groups (red arrows indicate mitochondria). Data are presented as mean ± SD. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

In vivo biocompatibility of C-dots-T7. (a) H&E-stained images of major organ sections from mice 7 days after different treatments. (b–i) Blood routine and biochemical parameters of mice 7 days after different treatments (n = 3, independent samples). Data are presented as mean ± SD.

Neurological function recovery of MCAO mice treated with C-dots-T7. (a) Schematic illustration of the neurological function recovery assessment protocol for MCAO mice. (b) Body weight of mice in each treatment group. (c) Movement trajectories of mice in the open field test across different treatment groups. (d) Average speed of mice in the open field test for each group. (e) Average movement distance of mice in the open field test across different treatment groups. (f) Forelimb asymmetry ratio in the cylinder test for each group. (g) Time to contact adhesive tape in each treatment group. (h) Time to remove adhesive tape across different treatment groups. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001.

RNA-seq analysis of ischemic brain tissue following treatment with C-dots-T7 and PBS. (a) Heatmap of differentially expressed genes (DEGs) between PBS and C-dots-T7 groups (DEGs; fold change >2 and p-value < 0.05). (b) Volcano plot of all DEGs between PBS and C-dots-T7 groups, where each dot represents a DEG (fold change >2 and p-value < 0.05); red indicates upregulated genes, and green indicates downregulated genes. (c) Pathways of the top 30 significantly DEGs identified by Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA), with Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) used as the gene set of interest. GSEA enrichment maps of DEGs are focused on the regulation of (d) Oxidative phosphorylation, (e) mTOR signaling pathway, and (f) HIF-1 signaling pathway. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Engineering ultrasmall red fluorescent carbon dots for targeted blood-brain barrier crossing, oxidative stress alleviation and mitochondrial repair in cerebral ischemia-reperfusion injury treatment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The alterations in the brain microenvironment caused by reperfusion following acute ischemic stroke are significant obstacles to neurological recovery and a key factor contributing to stroke recurrence after thrombolytic therapy. Elevated levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the brain contribute to both vascular and neuronal damage, underscoring the critical need for the rational design of effective antioxidants with high biocompatibility and efficiency. In this work, we design and engineer a distinct fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots-T7) with specific targeting capabilities by conjugating the T7 peptide (HAIYPRH) for a high affinity to apoptotic neurons. The synthesized C-dots-T7 shows stable red fluorescence with an emission peak at approximately 683 nm, enabling effective in vitro and in vivo bioimaging. In vivo animal experiments demonstrate that the ultrasmall C-dots-T7 (approximately 2–5 nm) efficiently cross the blood-brain barrier, target and accumulate in the ischemic brain tissues, and significantly reduce brain infarct volume by approximately 57.7 % compared to the untreated group in a middle cerebral artery occlusion (MCAO) model. Importantly, the C-dots-T7 can accumulate within mitochondria, activate the oxidative phosphorylation pathway to alleviate mitochondrial dysfunction, and inhibit the mTOR and HIF-1 signaling pathways to prevent excessive cellular damage caused by oxidative stress. This work demonstrates that C-dots-T7 serve as a high-performance nanosystem for fluorescence imaging and targeted treatment of cerebral ischemia-reperfusion injury, with high clinical translation potential.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Stroke, characterized by the sudden interruption of blood supply or hemorrhage in the brain, leads to brain tissue damage and triggers a series of neurological dysfunctions. It has become one of the leading causes of mortality and disability worldwide [1,2]. The standard treatment for acute ischemic stroke involves the administration of tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) to dissolve the thrombus, restore cerebral blood flow, and mitigate neurological damage [3,4]. However, the therapeutic window for intravenous thrombolysis is limited to within 4.5 h after the onset of acute ischemic stroke, which significantly restricts the application of tPA [5]. Additionally, even when the occluded vessel is successfully recanalized, reperfusion injury becomes an inevitable secondary assault on the patient, making the reduction of ischemia-reperfusion injury a key focus in the treatment of ischemic stroke [6]. Vascular endothelial cell damage resulting from reperfusion following vessel recanalization can lead to hemorrhagic transformation, a critical complication of reperfusion therapy. Hemorrhagic transformation dramatically increases the mortality rate in stroke patients [7]. Therefore, the pathological mechanisms leading to brain injury following ischemic stroke are complex and multifaceted. During the ischemic phase, irreversible damage such as mitochondrial dysfunction, neuronal death, and inflammation activation due to the reduction of energy and oxygen supply occurs. The only effective strategy to mitigate this ischemic injury is to swiftly restore blood flow to the occluded vessels [8,9]. However, after reperfusion, mitochondrial dysfunction triggers an imbalance between oxidative and antioxidative processes [6], disrupting the equilibrium between the production and clearance of reactive oxygen species (ROS). This disruption leads to the excessive accumulation of ROS, exacerbating inflammation and further aggravating brain injury [10].

Nanozymes, nanomaterials with enzyme-like activities, have garnered significant attention in recent years due to their superior stability, cost-effectiveness, and tunable catalytic properties [11]. These characteristics make nanozymes promising alternatives to natural enzymes [12]. By integrating properties such as fluorescence and magnetism, nanozymes have demonstrated immense potential in applications like bioimaging, therapy, and diagnostics [13–15]. To date, a wide range of superoxide dismutase (SOD) nanozymes have been explored for the treatment of conditions such as sepsis, Alzheimer's disease, oral ulcers, cardiovascular diseases, COVID-19, inflammation, and injuries [16,17]. Carbon nanodots (C-dots) are a class of fluorescent nanomaterials with sizes smaller than 10 nm [18]. Since their discovery in 2004, C-dots have gained increasing attention due to their high stability, tunable emission, non-blinking properties, low cost, and excellent biocompatibility. These characteristics position C-dots as highly promising candidates for applications in sensing, bioimaging, thermosensitive and optoelectronic devices, among others [19,20]. Previous research has demonstrated that C-dots exhibit remarkable SOD nanozyme activity, and their optimal fluorescence emission wavelength is 682 nm, These features enable their effective use in sensitive fluorescence detection, with notable advantages in imaging biological tissues at greater depths and in living organisms [21,22]. Furthermore, various strategies have been developed to enhance the luminescence, stability, and biological functionality of C-dots. For example, selenium-doped carbon dots synthesized via process-intensified methods show outstanding free radical scavenging performance and excellent cell imaging capability with minimal cytotoxicity [23]. Similarly, multifunctional C-dot-based hydrogels have been fabricated for antioxidant and antibacterial wound treatment applications [24]. In parallel, quantum dot nanocomposites have also been engineered for optoelectronic textiles and wearable bioimaging devices [25]. In addition, recent efforts to develop highly stable, brightly emitting perovskite quantum dots, such as CH3NH3PbX3, provide further support for the fluorescence tunability and integration of nanomaterials in biological systems [26].

At present, most drugs exhibit limited targeting efficiency towards mitochondria in the brain parenchyma, which significantly affects the therapeutic outcomes for central nervous system (CNS) diseases [27]. Typically, very few macromolecular or small-molecule drugs are able to reach the CNS [28,29]. This is primarily due to several factors, including reduced drug permeability across the blood-brain barrier (BBB), the lipophilicity of the drugs, and the high negative potential of mitochondria [30]. These challenges substantially limit the targeting efficiency of therapeutic drugs to brain and neuronal mitochondria. Consequently, achieving therapeutic drug levels in the brain often requires high doses of systemic administration, which can lead to adverse side effects [20]. A specific ligand peptide, HAIYPRH (T7), has been shown to bind selectively to the transferrin receptor, which is overexpressed on brain capillary endothelial cells [31,32]. This interaction mediates the transport of nanocarriers across the BBB [33]. Recently, T7-conjugated nanoparticles have been reported for stroke therapy, demonstrating excellent targeting capability to ischemic regions of the brain [34].

In this study, we design a novel nanotherapeutic agent, C-dots-T7, by covalently linking a T7 targeting peptide to ultrasmall red fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) with high superoxide dismutase (SOD)-like activity and desirable biocompatibility. Compared with conventional antioxidants or nanozymes, the engineered C-dots-T7 nanozyme addresses the current critical challenge of poor therapeutic accumulation and limited targeting efficiency in ischemic regions of the brain. This targeted delivery strategy significantly reduces the severe side effects associated with high-dose systemic administration. The C-dots-T7 nanozyme exhibits optimal fluorescence emission at a wavelength of 682 nm, making it suitable for bioimaging applications, particularly for in vivo deep tissue imaging. After intravenous injection, C-dots-T7 efficiently cross the blood-brain barrier by actively targeting and binding to endothelial cells overexpressing transferrin receptors, an essential process often neglected or insufficiently addressed by previous studies. Once localized to ischemic regions, C-dots-T7 specifically accumulate within mitochondria, effectively scavenging excessive ROS, activating the oxidative phosphorylation pathway to alleviate mitochondrial dysfunction, and simultaneously inhibiting the mTOR and HIF-1 signaling pathways to reduce oxidative stress-induced neuronal damage. This process ultimately helps to protect and rescue neurons, offering new prospects for the application of ultrasmall fluorescent carbon dots in stroke treatment (Fig. 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design, preparation and characterization of ultrasmall C-dots-T7
ce:section.ce:para: This study successfully constructs C-dots-T7 according to the preparation protocol illustrated in Fig. 1. Ultrasmall red fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) are synthesized by dissolving glutathione and folic acid in formamide, heating the mixture at 160 °C for 8 h, followed by dialysis and ultrafiltration [35]. Glutathione provides sulfur and nitrogen heteroatoms, enhancing the SOD-like activity and antioxidant capacity of the C-dots. Folic acid offers a conjugated structure and additional carboxyl groups, improving red fluorescence and surface functionality [35]. Subsequently, the carboxyl groups on the C-dots are activated by stirring with 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS) at room temperature for 30 min, enabling them to undergo a dehydration condensation reaction with the amino groups on the T7 peptide [36]. The T7 peptide is then added to the reaction mixture and stirred for an additional 12 h to covalently conjugate it to the carboxyl-rich surface of the C-dots. Finally, the resulting C-dots-T7 conjugates are purified by dialysis and collected by lyophilization.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to observe the morphology, size distribution, and crystalline features of the synthesized C-dots and C-dots-T7. This technique enables direct visualization of structural differences before and after surface modification. TEM images reveal that the C-dots are well-dispersed, with particle sizes ranging from 1 to 3 nm. In contrast, the particle diameters of C-dots-T7 are approximately 2 to 5 nm, and white peptide rings are visible on their surface. Both C-dots and C-dots-T7 show crystallinity with lattice spacings of 0.21 nm (Fig. 2a). In addition, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, zeta potential analysis, and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy are employed to further analyze the obtained compounds, aiming to confirm the successful conjugation of T7 peptide with the C-dots. UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy is employed to analyze the effect of varying T7 peptide modification ratios on the optical properties of C-dots and to verify the success of surface conjugation. Firstly, in the 190–200 nm region, an increase in absorbance is evident with rising concentrations of T7 (blue curve representing low concentration and yellow curve representing high concentration). This suggests that the unique UV absorption characteristics of the T7 molecules significantly contribute to this wavelength range, likely due to the presence of aromatic structures or conjugated systems within the T7. Secondly, a strong absorption peak appears in the 380–420 nm range, which may be attributed to surface states of the C-dots or π-π transitions. The modification with T7 has a relatively minor effect on this peak. Lastly, the absorption peaks in the 600–700 nm range may be related to defect states in the C-dots or π-π transitions. The absorption in this region remains largely unchanged after T7 modification, indicating that the introduction of T7 does not significantly alter the long-wavelength absorption behavior of the C-dots. These changes in absorbance reveal the regulatory effect of T7 molecules on the optical properties of C-dots, particularly in the UV region (Fig. 2b).

Zeta potential analysis is performed to evaluate changes in the surface charge of nanomaterials, thereby indirectly confirming the successful modification of C-dots with T7 peptide. After modification, the surface charge of the C-dots-T7 nanocomplex increases to an average of approximately −7.58 mV (Fig. 2c). To confirm the successful conjugation of T7 peptide to the surface of C-dots at the molecular level, FTIR spectroscopy is employed to identify changes in characteristic functional groups and the formation of new chemical bonds. Covalent attachment of peptides (e.g. T7 or other amine-containing peptides) onto C-dots via EDC/NHS chemistry is evidenced by the appearance of new amide bond signals in FTIR. After such bioconjugation, new peaks emerge at ∼1670 cm−1 (amide I CO stretch) and ∼ 1550 cm−1 (amide II NH bend), which are absent in unmodified C-dots, confirming that peptide coupling via amide bond formation has occurred [37,38]. This indicates the successful formation of amide bonds from the T7 molecules. Additionally, a broadened and intensified absorption peak is observed in the 3300–3500 cm−1 region, which is associated with the amine or amide groups present in T7 [39]. This further supports the successful conjugation of T7 molecules onto the C-dots surface (Fig. 2d). Therefore, the TEM, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, zeta potential, and FT-IR collectively confirm the successful covalent bonding between C-dots and T7.

Fluorescence characterization and lifetime measurements are conducted. These analyses aim to verify whether the fluorescence emission and decay dynamics are preserved after conjugation. The C-dots solution exhibits a green appearance and emits red fluorescence, with a distinct excitation peak at 420 nm and a prominent emission peak at 683 nm (Fig. 2e). Similarly, C-dots-T7 aqueous solutions emit a distinct red fluorescence under UV light (365 nm) illumination (Fig. S1). By comparing the fluorescence lifetime curves of C-dots (red curve) and C-dots-T7 (blue curve), it can be observed that their fluorescence decay behaviors are very similar. Both nanosystems exhibit biexponential decay. The primary lifetime constants for C-dots are τ1 = 5.48 ns and τ2 = 20.00 ns, while for C-dots-T7, the primary lifetime constants are τ1 = 5.71 ns and τ2 = 19.63 ns. The data indicate that despite T7 modification slightly affects the fluorescence lifetime of C-dots, resulting in a minor extension, the overall decay trend remains largely unchanged. This suggests that after modification, the fluorescence decay behavior of C-dots-T7 remains comparable to that of unmodified C-dots, indicating that the primary fluorescence decay mechanism is not significantly altered by the T7 modification (Fig. 2f). Furthermore, the hemolysis assay results demonstrated that C-dots-T7 exhibit a hemolysis rate close to 0 % across the concentration range of 75 to 600 μg/mL. This indicates that the engineered C-dots-T7 nanosystem does not cause significant damage to red blood cells at these concentrations and possesses desirable hemocompatibility (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro enzyme-mimicking and ROS-scavenging performance of C-dots-T7
ce:section.ce:para: The imbalance between the production and clearance of reactive oxygen species (ROS) leads to oxidative stress and excessive inflammatory responses, exacerbating damage in ischemic regions [40]. In light of this, we first investigate the ability of C-dots-T7 to scavenge various types of ROS. DPPH and ABTS radicals are employed as in vitro radical models to assess the radical scavenging capacity of C-dots-T7 as a nanozyme. As the concentration of DPPH radicals increases from 25 μg/mL to 150 μg/mL, the inhibition rate of DPPH radicals shows a significant rise, reaching a maximum inhibition of approximately 80 % at the highest concentration (150 μg/mL). This result indicates that the sample exhibits strong antioxidant activity against DPPH radicals, with higher concentrations leading to more effective radical scavenging (Fig. S3). C-dots-T7 also exhibits strong antioxidant activity against ABTS radicals. At lower concentrations (0–20 μg/mL), the inhibition rate increases sharply. As the concentration continues to rise, the inhibition rate further increases, albeit at a slower pace, indicating a saturation effect. At 100 μg/mL, the inhibition rate approaches its maximum, suggesting that the majority of ABTS radicals have been neutralized (Fig. S4).

Superoxide anions (O2 −) can disrupt cellular structure and function. In living cells, superoxide dismutase (SOD) plays a critical role in maintaining redox balance by converting O2 − into hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and oxygen (O2) [41]. Therefore, the SOD-like enzyme activity of C-dots-T7 is preliminarily investigated. In this assay, oxygen is reduced to superoxide anions (O2 −) through riboflavin photoreduction, with methionine serving as the electron donor to enhance the reaction. The generated O2 − subsequently reduces nitro-blue tetrazolium (NBT) to form blue formazan, which is quantified by spectrophotometry at 560 nm [42]. As the concentration of C-dots-T7 nanozymes increases, the scavenging rate of O2 − significantly improves, indicating that C-dots-T7 features SOD-like activity. At a concentration of 80 μg/mL, the scavenging rate reaches 95 %, showing no significant difference compared to the SOD activity of unmodified C-dots (Fig. 2g).

Using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the spin trapping reagent, the SOD-like enzyme activity of C-dots-T7 is investigated through electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy [43]. The ESR spectrum demonstrates the effect of varying concentrations of C-dots-T7 on superoxide anions. The control group shows strong ESR signals, indicating a high presence of superoxide anions. As the concentration of C-dots-T7 increases (from 25 μg/mL to 100 μg/mL), the intensity of the ESR signals progressively decreases. At 25 μg/mL, the signal weakens significantly, and this trend continues with further reductions at 50 μg/mL. At 100 μg/mL, the ESR signal is almost entirely diminished, indicating that C-dots-T7 effectively scavenges superoxide anions in a dose-dependent manner, with the highest concentration providing the most significant antioxidant activity (Fig. 2h).

Hydroxyl radicals (OH) are another significant factor that causes cellular damage. They induce a range of negative effects, including DNA damage, protein oxidation, lipid peroxidation, and cell apoptosis [44]. C-dots-T7 effectively scavenges hydroxyl radicals (OH) in a dose-dependent manner. The inhibition rate increases from 0 % at 0 μg/mL to about 90 % at 300 μg/mL, demonstrating strong antioxidant activity at higher concentrations (Fig. 2i). The ESR analysis further confirms the OH scavenging ability of C-dots-T7. As the concentration of C-dots-T7 increases, the intensity of the ESR signal gradually weakens, and at a concentration of 200 μg/mL, the ESR signal was almost completely diminished (Fig. 2j). Therefore, the carbon dot-based nanozyme (C-dots-T7) with multiple catalytic activities is successfully developed. This nanozyme effectively scavenges DPPH radicals, ABTS radicals, superoxide anions (O2 −), and hydroxyl radicals (OH). It holds promise as a potential antioxidant for the treatment of ischemic stroke, alleviating the severe ROS microenvironment in ischemic brain regions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro targeting ability and therapeutic efficacy
ce:section.ce:para: SH-SY5Y cells are derived from neuroblastoma and were established in 1970 from a metastatic bone tumor of a 4-year-old cancer patient [45], SH-SY5Y cells are widely used in the fields of neurodegenerative disease research, neurodevelopment, and drug screening [1]. In this study, we employ an oxygen-glucose deprivation (OGD)-induced oxidative stress model to replicate stroke-specific cellular damage, which is widely recognized for its relevance to ischemic stroke [1].

Given the inherent fluorescent properties of C-dots-T7, we investigate their ability to enter neuronal cells. SH-SY5Y cells are incubated with C-dots-T7 (100 μg/mL) for different durations, followed by imaging with a laser confocal microscope. A maximum concentration of 100 μg/mL, at which there is no difference in cytotoxicity between C-dots and C-dots-T7 (Fig. S5), is selected to facilitate subsequent comparative and functional experiments. The results show that the accumulation of C-dots-T7 within the cells gradually increases over time, reaching saturation at 6 h, after which the nanomaterials are gradually expelled from the cells (Fig. 3a, b). As shown in Fig. 3c, after co-culturing SH-SY5Y cells with C-dots-T7, we also observe that C-dots-T7 had successfully internalized into the SH-SY5Y cells.

The T7 targeting peptide specifically recognizes and binds to transferrin receptor, which are particularly elevated after a stroke, especially during ischemia-reperfusion injury [46]. During this process, the BBB is compromised, and the T7 peptide, by targeting the transferrin receptor, helps drugs cross the BBB and preferentially accumulate in the damaged regions [47]. OGD cellular model is used to simulate the ischemic and hypoxic environment of brain ischemia in vitro. Taking advantage of the fluorescent properties of C-dots-T7, we perform fluorescence imaging to verify its specific targeting ability towards OGD-damaged cells. After incubating SH-SY5Y cells with different concentrations of C-dots-T7 for varying durations, flow cytometry analysis reveals that, compared to the control group, C-dots-T7 exhibits enhanced targeting efficacy towards OGD-damaged neurons, regardless of concentration (Figs. 3d, e). Additionally, co-culturing normal SH-SY5Y cells with the same concentration of C-dots and C-dots-T7 for varying durations reveals that SH-SY5Y cells exhibit comparable phagocytic abilities towards both. These findings demonstrate the specific targeting capability of C-dots-T7 for damaged cells (Fig. S6).

To ensure the safety of the nanomaterials, we first assess the cytotoxicity of C-dots and C-dots-T7 on SH-SY5Y cells after 24 h of incubation using the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay [48]. The results indicate that neither C-dots nor C-dots-T7 exhibit cytotoxicity towards SH-SY5Y cells, even with increasing concentrations, suggesting that these nanomaterials are safe for subsequent experiments (Fig. 3f). However, after co-culturing for 48 h, we find a significant decline in cell viability in the C-dots treated group, while the C-dots-T7 group maintains healthy cell status. This observation demonstrates that the modification of C-dots with the T7 targeting peptide effectively enhances their in vitro safety (Fig. S5). Following OGD induction, SH-SY5Y cells are treated with various concentrations of C-dots-T7 to evaluate their intracellular antioxidant capacity. Cell viability is then measured using the CCK-8 assay, and intracellular ROS accumulation is evaluated by flow cytometry using 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA) and dihydroethidium (DHE) as indicators for ROS and superoxide anions (O2 −), respectively. The CCK-8 results show that the survival rate of SH-SY5Y cells decreases to 68 % after OGD, whereas C-dots-T7 significantly improves cell viability within a certain concentration range, reaching a peak at 100 μg/mL with a survival rate of 84 % (Fig. 3i). The DCFH-DA fluorescence staining results indicate that ROS levels in OGD-induced SH-SY5Y cells are significantly reduced after treatment with different concentrations of C-dots-T7, with 40 μg/mL demonstrating a stronger ROS scavenging effect compared to 10 μg/mL (Fig. S7). DHE fluorescence staining is used to evaluate the ability of C-dots-T7 to eliminate superoxide anions in living cells. Flow cytometry confirms that the DHE fluorescence intensity within SH-SY5Y cells is markedly reduced after C-dots-T7 treatment (Fig. S8). These findings collectively suggest that C-dots-T7 protects living cells from ROS-induced damage by scavenging superoxide anions.

Mitochondria are the primary organelles responsible for generating endogenous ROS, and mitochondrial damage is a key factor that induces ROS levels to exceed toxic thresholds during ischemia-reperfusion injury [49]. We investigate whether C-dots-T7 can enter mitochondria by incubating SH-SY5Y cells with C-dots-T7 for 12 h, followed by mitochondrial staining with Mito-Tracker Green. As shown in Fig. 3c, the Pearson's correlation coefficient between the red (C-dots-T7) and green (Mito-Tracker) channels in the provided image is approximately 0.87. This indicates a strong positive correlation between the red and green signals, suggesting a significant degree of colocalization of C-dots-T7 with mitochondria in SH-SY5Y cells.

Mito-SOX is a fluorescent probe specifically designed to detect superoxide anions (O₂−) in mitochondria [50]. It selectively targets the mitochondria of living cells and reacts specifically with mitochondrial superoxide anions, generating a red fluorescent product. After treating OGD-induced SH-SY5Y cells with different concentrations of C-dots-T7, we use Mito-SOX to measure the levels of O₂− in mitochondria. Following OGD treatment, the fluorescence intensity in cells significantly increases, indicating elevated ROS levels. However, with the help of C-dots-T7, the fluorescence intensity in OGD-treated cells is markedly reduced, demonstrating that C-dots-T7 has the ability to scavenge O₂− in mitochondria of living cells (Figs. 3g, h). Additionally, JC-1 staining is employed to assess mitochondrial membrane potential, which reflects mitochondrial function. This method allows us to further evaluate the impact of C-dots-T7 on mitochondrial function in OGD-modeled cells. After OGD, the mitochondrial membrane potential in SH-SY5Y cells significantly decreases, but treatment with various concentrations of C-dots-T7 notably reverses this decline. The effect is more pronounced at 40 μg/mL compared to 10 μg/mL (Figs. 3j, k). Based on these results, we can conclude that ROS induced by the OGD model promotes cell death, while C-dots-T7, through its ability to target cellular mitochondria and scavenge ROS, maintains mitochondrial integrity, thereby reducing endogenous ROS release and ultimately improving cell survival.

To evaluate the delivery, distribution, and targeted retention capabilities of the auto-fluorescent nanozyme C-dots-T7 in ischemic brain tissue, we establish the typical MCAO (middle cerebral artery occlusion) mouse model [51,52] (Fig. 4a). In brief, this model involves inserting a filament into the middle cerebral artery of mice for 60 min, followed by removal of the filament to allow reperfusion (Fig. S9). C-dots and C-dots-T7 are intravenously injected into MCAO model mice, and brain samples are collected at 3, 6, 12, and 24 h post-injection for fluorescence imaging (Fig. 4b). At 6 h post-injection, the fluorescence intensity in the brains of MCAO mice reaches its peak and remains at a high level for up to 24 h. This indicates that the accumulation of C-dots-T7 in ischemic brain lesions is time-dependent. In contrast, only weak fluorescence is observed in the brains of mice injected with unmodified C-dots. At 6 h, the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots-T7 group is 254 % that of the C-dots group (Fig. 4d), indicating that T7 peptide modification improves brain-targeted delivery of the C-dots. Further analysis of brain sections from each group reveals that the ipsilateral brain (left side) in the C-dots-T7 group exhibits significantly higher fluorescence signals, with a 242 % increase compared to the contralateral brain at 6 h. In contrast, there is little difference in fluorescence intensity between the two brain regions in the C-dots group (Figs. 4c, d), This phenomenon clearly demonstrates the enhanced targeting capability of C-dots-T7 towards brain lesions.

In another study, major organs are collected at different time points after intravenous injection of C-dots and C-dots-T7 into mice to further elucidate their biodistribution in vivo (Fig. 4e). The fluorescence intensity in various organs reaches its highest level at 6 h and then gradually declines. However, the clearance rate of C-dots-T7 is significantly slower than that of C-dots, likely attributable to the T7 peptide modification, which targets the transferrin receptor and thereby extends the nanoparticles' retention time within the body (Figs. 4f, g). Additionally, immunostaining for cluster of differentiation 31 (CD31), an endothelial cell marker, is performed on brain sections from the MCAO animal model (Fig. 4h). The goal of using CD31 immunostaining is to specifically label cerebral vascular endothelial cells, allowing us to visualize the localization and colocalization of C-dots-T7 with brain vasculature. Abundant fluorescence signals of C-dots-T7 are observed in the ipsilateral brain, with the signals distributed separately from CD31-positive endothelial cells. This distribution pattern suggests that the nanozyme efficiently crossed the BBB and accumulated within the ischemic brain parenchyma.

To verify the neuroprotective effects of C-dots-T7 in vivo against ischemia-reperfusion injury, we subject MCAO mice to different treatments, including PBS, C-dots (5 mg/kg), and C-dots-T7 (5 mg/kg). 24 h after treatment, brain infarct volumes in the different treatment groups are assessed using 2,3,5-triphenyltetrazolium chloride (TTC) staining to evaluate the protective effects of C-dots-T7 against ischemia-reperfusion injury (Fig. 5a). Compared to the PBS (48.2 %) and C-dots (36.4 %) groups, the mice treated with C-dots-T7 exhibit significantly smaller brain infarct volumes (20.4 %), indicating that C-dots-T7 more effectively reduces brain tissue damage than the untreated and non-targeted nanozyme groups (Fig. 5b). Neurological function deficit scores are determined using the Zea-Longa scoring system. The results show that C-dots-T7 significantly reduces neurological function deficit scores (Fig. 5c). TUNEL staining is performed to assess the apoptotic status of cells in the ischemic region following different treatments. Compared to the PBS treatment group, the apoptotic rate of TUNEL-positive cells in the hippocampus of mice treated with C-dots and C-dots-T7 is significantly reduced, indicating that the spontaneously fluorescent nano-enzyme offers substantial neuroprotection in mice with cerebral ischemia (Fig. 5d).

Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining is utilized to examine the pathological changes in ischemic brain sections. In contrast to the sham group, the PBS treatment group exhibits significant morphological alterations, with disrupted cellular and tissue structures. Following treatment with C-dots and C-dots-T7, the cellular morphology shows a tendency to resemble that of the sham group, demonstrating the effective therapeutic potential of the spontaneously fluorescent nano-enzyme against pathological damage caused by cerebral ischemic stroke (Fig. 5e, f). Nissl staining is employed to label Nissl bodies in the cortex and hippocampus, allowing for the evaluation of neuronal functional status following different treatments. MCAO significantly induces neuronal necrosis in the hippocampal region, resulting in a reduced number of normal neurons. Damaged neurons display shrinkage and deformity. Notably, treatment with C-dots and C-dots-T7 significantly salvages hippocampal neurons, markedly improving the pathological changes of neurons post-MCAO, preserving neuronal morphology, and increasing the number of surviving neurons (Fig. 5g, h).

Mitochondria serve as the main source of O2 −, which are generated during energy metabolism via the electron transport chain. Under normal physiological conditions, the ROS produced by mitochondria play a vital role in cellular signaling and metabolic regulation [53]. However, under stress conditions such as ischemia and reperfusion, mitochondrial damage can lead to elevated ROS levels, resulting in oxidative stress and cellular injury [54]. We perform DHE staining on brain slices from mice treated with C-dots and C-dots-T7 to detect O2 − in the brain (Fig. S10). The regular and dense cristae structure is essential for maintaining mitochondrial function [55]. We examine the ultrastructure of mitochondria (indicated by red arrows) 24 h post-MCAO using electron microscopy (Fig. 5i). Mitochondria in the sham group display dense cristae structures. In contrast, following MCAO injury, the cristae structure of the mitochondria is severely disrupted, leading to mitochondrial swelling and dissolution, indicating significant mitochondrial damage and irregular morphology. After treatment with C-dots or C-dots-T7, the cristae structures of the mitochondria are predominantly preserved, with the C-dots-T7 group exhibiting nearly normal physiological morphology. These data suggest that C-dots-T7 may alleviates the ultrastructural damage to mitochondria in the ischemic brain tissue following MCAO, thereby improving the integrity of the nervous system.

Additionally, the potential toxicity of therapeutic nanomedicine is a major obstacle to their application in the biomedical field, making it essential to perform biocompatibility tests to evaluate the toxicity of C-dots-T7 [56]. Seven days after the injection of C-dots and C-dots-T7, mice are sacrificed to collect major organs and serum samples. H&E staining of major organs (heart, lungs, liver, spleen, kidneys, and brain) demonstrates that C-dots and C-dots-T7 cause no tissue toxicity or significant differences (Fig. 6a). Systemic toxicity is also evaluated by analyzing serum biochemical parameters and routine blood indices, including liver function markers aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and albumin (ALB), kidney markers urea nitrogen (UREA) and creatinine (CREA), immune-related markers lymphocytes (LYM) and granulocytes (GRAN), and hematological markers red blood cells (RBC) and platelets (PLT) [5]. Fig. 6b–i show that the levels of these markers in the serum of treated mice are normal compared to the sham group, indicating that neither C-dots-T7 nor C-dots cause any organ dysfunction. In conclusion, these findings suggest that C-dots-T7 exhibits excellent biocompatibility and is suitable for biological research applications.

To further evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of C-dots-T7 against ischemic brain injury in the MCAO mouse model (Fig. 7a), we closely monitor the body weight of mice treated with PBS, C-dots (5 mg/kg), and C-dots-T7 (5 mg/kg), to assess the neuroprotective effects on the central nervous system following ischemic stroke (Fig. 7b). Compared to the sham group, all MCAO-treated mice exhibit a decrease in body weight, with the PBS-treated mice showing the most significant weight loss and no trend of recovery. In contrast, the MCAO mice treated with C-dots-T7 demonstrate a trend towards recovery after an initial decline in body weight, indicating a significant protective effect of C-dots-T7 against ischemia-reperfusion injury. Additionally, the survival rate of MCAO mice treated with C-dots-T7 is markedly higher compared to those receiving PBS or C-dots treatment, further confirming the neuroprotective role of C-dots-T7 in ischemic brain injury (Fig. S11).

To evaluate the recovery of motor function in MCAO mice following treatment with C-dots-T7, representative open field tests are conducted on days 1, 3, 5, and 7 post-MCAO (Fig. 7c). Compared to the sham group, all treatment groups exhibit significant motor impairments, consistent with reduced activity and motor dysfunction due to unilateral paralysis following stroke [57]. Notably, after 7 days of C-dots-T7 treatment, there is a marked improvement in the motor abilities of the mice, with significant differences observed in average speed and distance traveled (Fig. 7d, e). Asymmetry in forelimb usage is assessed using the cylinder and adhesive removal tests. After 7 days of C-dots-T7 treatment, MCAO mice show a significant reduction in forelimb asymmetry during the cylinder test compared to the PBS and C-dots groups (Fig. 7f). Additionally, the time taken to contact and remove adhesive tape in the adhesive removal test is significantly decreased, indicating that C-dots-T7 treatment effectively ameliorates the unilateral paralysis induced by MCAO (Fig. 7g, h). These results suggest that C-dots-T7 treatment significantly enhances behavioral function and promotes neurological recovery in MCAO mice following ischemic stroke.

Approximately 40 % to 80 % of stroke patients experience unilateral sensory loss [58]. While some patients may exhibit spontaneous recovery during the acute phase of stroke, persistent deficits can lead to lasting damage beyond this period [59]. However, our findings suggest that the administration of C-dots-T7 helps to prevent or reverse unilateral sensory loss. In summary, our results indicate that C-dots-T7 can directly target the damaged areas of the brain via the bloodstream, resulting in comprehensive improvements in motor skills in mice affected by stroke.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on molecular mechanisms by RNA sequencing analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the molecular mechanisms underlying the therapeutic effects of C-dots-T7 on ischemic stroke, we perform RNA sequencing analysis on brain tissues from PBS and C-dots-T7 groups 24 h post-MCAO to evaluate gene expression regulation and identify differentially expressed genes (DEGs). We identify 22,508 and 22,093 genes in the PBS and C-dots-T7 groups, respectively. The transcriptomic heatmap indicates a distinct mRNA expression profile in the brain tissues of MCAO mice treated with C-dots-T7 compared to the PBS group (Fig. 8a). Genes with a log2 fold change ≥1.0 are considered significantly upregulated, while those with <−1.0 are considered downregulated (treatment vs. control group). Compared to the PBS group, we identify 152 DEGs in the C-dots-T7 group, including 102 upregulated genes (red dots) and 50 downregulated genes (green dots) (Fig. 8b).

The aforementioned results have demonstrated that C-dots-T7 exhibits multiple neuroprotective functions, including reducing oxidative stress and modulating inflammatory responses. To further elucidate the biological functions altered by C-dots-T7, we employ bioinformatics approaches to comprehensively analyze these changes. Using clusterProfiler for GSEA enrichment analysis of the differentially expressed genes (DEGs) and selecting KEGG as the gene set of interest, we identify overarching functional pathway alterations and pinpointed key gene sets associated with phenotypic changes [60]. Compared to the PBS group, multiple signaling pathways in the C-dots-T7 group, including the HIF-1 signaling pathway, oxidative phosphorylation, mTOR signaling pathway, and calcium signaling pathway, are notably affected, aligning with mitochondrial therapy, neuroprotection, and anti-inflammatory mechanisms observed in vitro and in vivo (Fig. 8c). Specifically, C-dots-T7 markedly activates the oxidative phosphorylation pathway, which plays a critical role in cellular metabolic balance, energy supply, and redox homeostasis [61], Activation of oxidative phosphorylation significantly alleviates mitochondrial dysfunction (Fig. 8d). Meanwhile, inhibition of the mTOR pathway reduces inflammatory responses and decreases activation of microglia and astrocytes, thus minimizing neuronal damage [62] (Fig. 8e). During ischemic stroke, thrombus formation reduces oxygen supply to brain tissue, prompting activation of specific cellular oxygen sensors, particularly the HIF-1 signaling pathway, to adapt to hypoxic conditions [63]. C-dots-T7 effectively inhibits the excessive activation of the HIF-1 pathway, mitigating oxidative stress during reperfusion (Fig. 8f). Concurrently, we conduct GSEA enrichment analysis, utilizing Reactome as the gene set of interest. We also find that treatment with C-dots-T7 activates several signaling pathways, including the citric acid (TCA) cycle and respiratory electron transport, which are critical pathways for mitochondrial function (Fig. S12). These pathway-level insights from sequencing results suggest a molecular mechanism for the neuroprotective effects of C-dots-T7, providing further evidence for the nanozyme's therapeutic potential in ischemic stroke treatment.

In this study, we have designed and engineered a C-dots-T7 fluorescent nanozyme with the distinct mitochondrial recovery and antioxidant activity. TEM analysis confirms that the T7 modification maintains the ultrasmall structure (2–5 nm) of C-dots. In vitro experiments reveal that C-dots-T7 effectively scavenge approximately 95 % of superoxide anions at a concentration of 80 μg/mL, thereby enhancing neuronal survival. As a nanozyme with antioxidant properties, C-dots-T7 can directly target mitochondria in vitro, alleviating mitochondrial dysfunction by scavenging superoxide anions around the mitochondria and ultimately rescuing cells. More importantly, in vivo experiments demonstrated that T7 modification significantly enhanced the targeting specificity of C-dots for ischemic brain regions, resulting in approximately 2.5-fold higher fluorescence intensity compared to unmodified C-dots at 6 h post-injection in the MCAO model. Correspondingly, the targeted accumulation of C-dots-T7 effectively crossed the blood-brain barrier and specifically localized in ischemic tissues, leading to a remarkable reduction in brain infarct volume by approximately 57.7 % compared to the PBS-treated group. Mechanistically, transcriptomic analysis indicated significant activation of the oxidative phosphorylation pathway and inhibition of the mTOR and HIF-1 signaling pathways, contributing to improved neuronal recovery and reduced inflammation in ischemic brain regions.

In summary, C-dots-T7 exhibit excellent bioimaging capability (fluorescence emission peak at 683 nm), enhanced targeting efficiency (∼2.5-fold increase in ischemic accumulation), potent antioxidant activity (∼95 % superoxide scavenging rate), and favorable biocompatibility (negligible hemolysis rate at concentrations up to 600 μg/mL). This innovative nanozyme strategy not only offers a new perspective for the future development of fluorescent nanozymes, but also presents a promising therapeutic avenue for clinical ischemic stroke treatment.

T7 (HAIYPRH) peptide was purchased from Beijing Zhongke Yaguang Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Glutathione (reduced), formamide, DPPH, ABTS, potassium persulfate (K₂S₂O₈), methionine, riboflavin, nitro blue tetrazolium (NBT), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC), ferrous sulfate (FeSO₄), and salicylic acid were obtained from Aladdin Holdings Group Co., Ltd. Folic acid and N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS) were sourced from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., and hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was acquired from Biosharp. SH-SY5Y cells, specialized SH-SY5Y cell culture medium, CCK-8, and glucose-free DMEM were obtained from Wuhan Pricella Biotechnology Co., Ltd. ROS assay kits, DHE assay kits, JC-1 assay kits, Mito-SOX assay kits, and mitochondrial staining kits were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology. Anaerobic culture bags and anaerobic gas packs were sourced from Mitsubishi (Japan). MCAO thread embolism was acquired from Beijing Sinon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Regenerated cellulose dialysis bags (3500 Da) were obtained from Shanghai YuanYe Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Ultrafiltration tubes (15 mL, 3 kDa) were sourced from Millipore.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a JEM-F200 (JEOL, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using a UV-1800 spectrophotometer (MAPADA, China). FT-IR spectra were obtained using a Fourier infrared spectrometer (VERTEX70, Bruker Hong Kong Limited). Flow cytometric analysis for cellular uptake, intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, and dihydroethidium (DHE) generation was conducted using a flow cytometer (Beckman, USA). Zeta potential measurements were carried out using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). In vitro generation of superoxide radicals (O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (OH) was measured using an EMXplus electron spin resonance (ESR) spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). In vivo biodistribution analysis was conducted using an in vivo fluorescence imaging system (PerkinElmer, USA) [36].

2.1 g of glutathione and 0.105 g of folic acid were added to 70 mL of formamide. The mixture was reacted in an autoclave at 160 °C for 8 h. The resulting solution was first centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min to remove the precipitate, and the supernatant was dialyzed using a 3500 Da dialysis bag for 5–7 days. The prepared C-dots solution was then ultrafiltered at 4000g for 10 min using a Millipore centrifugal filtration device with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) membrane of 100 kDa, effectively separating C-dots with a molecular weight of <100 kDa [35].

5 mg of C-dots, 5 mg of NHS-sulfo, and 12 mg of EDC were stirred for 30 min under light-protected conditions to activate the carboxyl groups. Subsequently, 5 mg of T7 peptide chain was added, and the mixture was stirred for an additional 12 h. After the reaction, the resulting solution is purified by dialysis with a 3500 Da MWCO dialysis bag for 12 h, and C-dots-T7 is collected and concentrated by lyophilization.

1 mg of DPPH solid was dissolved in 24 mL of anhydrous ethanol, followed by ultrasonication for 5 min to achieve uniform dispersion. The resulting DPPH solution was then kept in the dark to prepare the working solution. C-dots-T7 samples were diluted with ethanol to prepare solutions of different concentrations. A mixture of 1.0 mL of the DPPH working solution and 0.5 mL of the sample solution was incubated in a 37 °C oven for 30 min. The absorbance was then measured at 517 nm using a microplate reader. The DPPH radical scavenging rate was calculated using the following formula: Scavenging rate (%) = [A0 − (Ax − Ax0)] / A0× 100 %, where A0 is the absorbance of the blank control, Ax is the absorbance of the sample, and Ax0 is the absorbance of the mixture without DPPH.

0.2 mL of 7.4 mmol/L ABTS and 0.2 mL of 2.6 mmol/L K2S2O8 were mixed and left in the dark at room temperature for 12 h. The resulting mixture was then diluted 10 times with PBS (pH 7.4) to prepare the ABTS radical working solution. A mixture of 0.8 mL of the ABTS radical working solution and 0.2 mL of C-dots-T7 at different concentrations was incubated in a 37 °C oven for 30 min. After incubation, the absorbance was measured at 734 nm using a microplate reader. The ABTS radical scavenging rate was calculated using the following formula: Scavenging rate (%) = [A0 − (Ax − Ax0)] / A0 × 100 %, where A0 is the absorbance of the blank control, Ax is the absorbance of the sample, and Ax0 is the absorbance of the mixture without ABTS.

Prepare a mixture of 300 μmol/L NBT, 50 mM/L methionine, 80 μmol/L riboflavin, and different concentrations of C-dots-T7 in a 1:1:1:1 ratio. After exposing the mixture to light for a certain period of time, immediately measure the absorbance at 560 nm using a microplate reader. The superoxide anion radical scavenging rate is calculated using the following formula: Scavenging rate (%) = [A0 − (Ax − Ax0)] / A0 × 100 %, where A0 is the absorbance of the blank control, Ax is the absorbance of the sample, and Ax0 is the absorbance of the mixture without NBT, methionine, or riboflavin [42].

Hydroxyl radicals were generated using the Fenton reaction. A mixture of 0.1 mL of 9 mmol/L FeSO4, 0.1 mL of 9 mmol/L salicylic acid, 0.1 mL of 8.8 mmol/L H2O2, and 1.2 mL of C-dots-T7 at different concentrations was prepared and thoroughly mixed. The mixture was then heated in a 37 °C water bath for 15 min. The absorbance was measured at 510 nm using a microplate reader. The hydroxyl radical scavenging rate was calculated using the following formula: Scavenging rate (%) = [A0 − (Ax − Ax0)] / A0 × 100 %, where A0 is the absorbance of the blank control, Ax is the absorbance of the sample, and Ax0 is the absorbance of the mixture without H₂O₂.

The free radicals were quantitatively estimated by the ESR signal of the free radical spin adduct using the peak-to-peak height of the line of the ESR spectrum. In order to scavenge hydroxyl radicals, a 5 mg/mL FeSO4 solution was prepared. A 200 μL aliquot of this solution was first combined with 20 μL of DMPO and 160 μL of deionized water, followed by the addition of 20 μL of 30 % H2O2. The mixture was thoroughly vortexed and allowed to react for 5 min. The resulting mixture was then subjected to ESR measurement, serving as the control group data. For the experimental groups, the 160 μL of deionized water was replaced with 160 μL of the respective sample solutions. To scavenge superoxide radicals, xanthine (10 mM) and xanthine oxidase (1 U/mL) solutions were prepared using PBS buffer as the solvent. For each assay, 100 μL of xanthine solution and 100 μL of xanthine oxidase solution were mixed with 20 μL of DMPO and 180 μL of PBS buffer. After a 10-min incubation period, the samples were collected for measurement. In the experimental groups, the 180 μL of PBS buffer was substituted with 180 μL of the respective sample solutions.

SH-SY5Y cells were obtained from Procell Life Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China). The cells were cultured in Procell's specialized SH-SY5Y medium, which contains 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS), 1 % penicillin-streptomycin, and 2 mM glutamine. Cells were maintained at 37 °C in a humidified incubator with 5 % CO2, and the culture medium was replaced every two days. When the cells reached approximately 80 % confluency, they were harvested using a 0.25 % trypsin solution.

The cytotoxicity of C-dots-T7 was evaluated using the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. Briefly, SH-SY5Y cells were seeded at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well in a 96-well plate and incubated at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for 24 h. After incubation, the medium was replaced with 100 μL of fresh medium containing varying concentrations of C-dots-T7. The cells were then incubated for an additional 24 h. Following this, the cells were gently washed with pre-warmed PBS. Subsequently, 100 μL of fresh DMEM containing 10 μL of CCK-8 reagent was added to each well, and the cells were incubated for another 30 min. The relative cell viability was determined by measuring the absorbance at 450 nm using a microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of oxygen-glucose deprivation (OGD) cell model
ce:section.ce:para: To simulate ischemia injury in vitro, an OGD model was established using SH-SY5Y cells. Briefly, SH-SY5Y cells were cultured in standard medium at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for 24 h. The cells were then washed with PBS and incubated in glucose-free DMEM. To induce oxygen-glucose deprivation (OGD), the cells were placed in an anaerobic culture bag containing an anaerobic gas pack and incubated for 4 h. Following the OGD period, the glucose-free medium was rapidly replaced with normal SH-SY5Y culture medium, and the cells were incubated under normoxic conditions (5 % CO2) for 12 h to simulate the reperfusion process [1].

To precisely monitor the uptake of C-dots-T7 by SH-SY5Y cells, cells were cultured in confocal dishes at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well. The cells were then incubated with 100 μg/mL of C-dots-T7 and observed at various time intervals using confocal microscopy.

To investigate the uptake behavior of C-dots-T7 in damaged SH-SY5Y cells, cells were seeded in six-well plates at a density of 2 × 105 cells per well. After 48 h of growth, a monolayer of cells was formed. The plates were then subjected to OGD treatment, and varying concentrations of C-dots-T7 were administered during the reperfusion period to facilitate their migration and penetration into the damaged cells. After 12 h, the fluorescence intensity of C-dots-T7 within the cells was quantitatively measured using flow cytometry (FCM).

To further evaluate the colocalization of C-dots-T7 with mitochondria in SH-SY5Y cells, the cells were cultured in confocal dishes at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well. After treatment with 100 μg/mL of C-dots-T7 for 12 h, the cells were washed with PBS twice. Mitochondria were stained using Mito-Tracker Green (Beyotime, China). The cells were then fixed using Antifade Mounting Medium with DAPI (Beyotime, China) and observed under a confocal laser scanning fluorescence microscope [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protective effects on SH-SY5Y cells following OGD treatment
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro, the protective effects of C-dots-T7 on SH-SY5Y cells following OGD treatment were evaluated through several assays, including cell viability assays, intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) staining, intracellular superoxide anion staining, mitochondrial membrane potential staining, and mitochondrial superoxide anion staining. Briefly, SH-SY5Y cells were cultured as monolayers in confocal dishes and six-well plates at the indicated densities, followed by OGD treatment. During the reperfusion phase, different concentrations of C-dots-T7 were added. After 12 h, the protective effects on SH-SY5Y cells were assessed using the CCK-8 assay, ROS assay kit (Beyotime), superoxide anion assay kit (Beyotime), and mitochondrial membrane potential assay kit (Beyotime) [64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Animals and establishment of MCAO model
ce:section.ce:para: Male C57BL/6 mice (8–10 weeks old, weighing 25–30 g) were purchased from Hangzhou Ziyuan Experimental Animal Technology Co., Ltd. and housed in a controlled environment with a temperature of 24 ± 2 °C and humidity of 55 ± 5 %, under a standard 12-h light/dark cycle. The mice were maintained under natural circadian rhythms with free access to food and water before and after the induction of intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH). All procedures involving mice were conducted in accordance with the guidelines and regulations of the Experimental Animal Center and the ethical guidelines of the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Shanghai University (Approval number: ECSHU 2021-029). Middle cerebral artery occlusion (MCAO) model was established to mimic cerebral ischemia/reperfusion injury in vivo. Briefly, C57BL/6 mice were anesthetized with an intraperitoneal injection of 4 % chloral hydrate at a dose of 10 mL/kg. The right common carotid artery (CCA), external carotid artery (ECA), and internal carotid artery (ICA) were exposed and isolated through a small neck incision. A nylon monofilament with a silicone-coated tip was gently inserted into the internal carotid artery via the common carotid artery to induce temporary occlusion of the right middle cerebral artery. After 1 h, the filament was removed to restore blood flow to the right middle cerebral artery. In the sham-operated group, the mice underwent the same surgical procedures without occlusion [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ischemic brain targeting of C-dots-T7 in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (C-dots) and C-dots-T7, which exhibit intrinsic fluorescence, were intravenously injected into MCAO mice at an equivalent C-dots dose of 5 mg/kg. The mice were euthanized at various time points post-injection, and the major organs and brain were imaged to assess the corresponding fluorescence intensities, revealing the biodistribution of C-dots and C-dots-T7. Specifically, fluorescence signals were measured in brain slices from both hemispheres of the MCAO mice [5]. In a separate study, MCAO mice were injected intravenously with C-dots-T7. Six hours post-injection, the mice were euthanized, and the brains were cryosectioned into 2 mm thick coronal slices. The frozen sections were subsequently subjected to immunofluorescent staining for CD31 and DAPI, and fluorescence in both hemispheres of the brain was visualized using confocal microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic effects in the MCAO mouse model
ce:section.ce:para: The successfully modeled mice were randomly divided into three groups: the sham group, the C-dots group, and the C-dots-T7 group. The mice received a single intravenous injection of C-dots or C-dots-T7 (equivalent C-dots dose of 5 mg/kg) or an equal volume of saline, administered 30 min after the MCAO procedure. Survival rates were assessed daily after drug administration, and representative behavioral tests, including the adhesive removal test, open field test, and cylinder test were conducted on days 1, 3, 5, and 7. In a separate study, MCAO mice were intravenously injected with PBS, C-dots, or C-dots-T7 (equivalent C-dots dose of 5 mg/kg) and euthanized 24 h post-treatment. Brain tissue was collected and sectioned into 2 mm thick coronal slices, followed by staining with 2 % 2,3,5-triphenyltetrazolium chloride (TTC) for 20 min [65]. Infarct volume was imaged and assessed using ImageJ. In another study, brain tissues were collected 24 h post-treatment for H&E, Nissl, TUNEL, and DHE staining. Immunohistochemical analysis of brain sections was also performed, and samples were visualized using confocal microscopy. ImageJ was used for the quantitative analysis of fluorescence intensity in all images throughout the study [66].

Total RNA was extracted from the ischemic brain tissue of MCAO mice treated with PBS or C-dots-T7. RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) was then performed by Hangzhou GeneCare Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The RNA-seq experimental workflow included sample quality assessment, library construction, quality control, and sequencing. RNA-seq was conducted on the Illumina NovaSeq 6000 sequencing platform to sequence the entire mRNA transcriptome of specific tissues or cells at a given time point in eukaryotic organisms. Library construction for the RNA-seq experiments was carried out using the Illumina TruSeq™ RNA Sample Prep Kit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility assessment of C-dots-T7 in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the safety of the nanoparticles, normal mice were randomly divided into three groups and intravenously injected with PBS, C-dots, or C-dots-T7 (equivalent C-dots dose of 5 mg/kg). Seven days after administration, liver and kidney function markers were assessed to reveal the toxicity of the different formulations to the liver and kidneys. Hematological analysis was also performed to evaluate the toxicity and infection risk associated with each formulation. Additionally, major organs were collected for H&E staining to assess the biosafety of the treatments [67].

All experiments were conducted using at least three independent replicates per experimental group (n ≥ 3). Statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism (version 9.5.0) or other genomic software. For normally distributed data with homogenous variances, comparisons between two groups were made using an independent t-test. For comparisons involving more than two groups, one-way or two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was employed. Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). In all figures, statistical significance is indicated as follows: ns for not significant; * for p < 0.05; ** for p < 0.01; *** for p < 0.001; and **** for p < 0.0001.
